speaker: interviewer
text: Date, February 7, 1995. Survivor, Michael Kaplan. Tape two. Mr. Kaplan, you were talking about being on the side of the road and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was sitting-- me and my younger brother were sitting on the side of the highway and we were eating bread. All of the sudden comes along my cousin, the cousin that we went to ask for help when we were going hungry. And he came along and said, oh, he was so happy to see us that, you know-- and he wanted to give me jewelry, or diamonds, or dollar bills. I should give him bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, Jakob, what are we going to do with this now? Nobody will take it. So I said, here's a half of bread. And OK, so he took the half of bread and we parted. His daughter still lives now in Brooklyn. Sometimes, we see her so she-- she remembers the-- the moment. But he, and his wife, and the other daughter-- he had two daughters-- all died in concentration camp. What happened after he left? You were now on the side of the road.

speaker: interviewee
text: He left. He went his way, and I went mine. Me and my younger brother, we went our way.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and what was your way? Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Turek. We went to Turek. And in Turek, I found my mother there. I don't know how she got there. We were so mixed up, tense. I forgot to ask her really how-- how she got to Turek. But in Turek, my-- my oldest-- older brother left his wife there. And we all-- I met all the brothers through there. Because we had in Turek was my mother's brother, also a baker.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the whole family were bakers. So we-- we-- we-- we congregate there. So me and my oldest brother, my youngest brother, and the other brother, we start marching towards Warsaw. Because there-- the Poles tried to talk us into that the Germans are so heavy, the equipment is so heavy, they get bogged down on the Polish highways and the dirt roads.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we believed them because, you know, we-- we didn't know anything else. And we walked for several days to-- to Warsaw. When we came before Warsaw, the Germans were already there. They encircled Warsaw and you couldn't get into town. So they send us back home, saying now you go back home. So we went back home. We went all the way back to-- to the town when we started out. And we went back to Kalisz, and back to our apartments and everything. And then the Germans started-- they-- they established themselves already, the administration. And they had these [NON-ENGLISH]. These are the Germans that live in Poland before the war. And they had Poles that helped them out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So well, every day was-- how should I say-- not a struggle, but what was a-- a winning day in you life. Because they catch you in the street, you don't know if you go home alive or not. Or they take you away by train, and you won't see your family anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a lot of things to talk about, but I want to highlight what they did in order to subjugate, in order to put a-- a-- a fear in the population that they should-- they shouldn't rise up or something, which was not a possibility at all. They-- they caught a furrier with his wife. And they round up the Jews, a couple hundred of them, in the market. They had-- they had to undress themself and perform a sex act. And we had to stay around around and watch it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you yourself there, or were you told this--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, hundreds of Jewish people. They round up Jewish people and just to see what they-- just-- just-- just like that. Then they-- a friend of mine, he had nice hair, black hair, nice boy. They cut across on-- in his hairline, across. And behind him, about 300 Jews had to march, like a funeral march. Then-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what else did they do? They hanged people in the-- in the-- [NON-ENGLISH], it was a store in the windows, [NON-ENGLISH], they hanged people right in there. So when you pass by, you see hanging.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see yourself somebody hanging, or were you just told about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I saw it. I saw it in Kalisz. It-- it-- it was-- it was the idea to-- to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to demoralize the people. Then they took the Jewish committee, the people that run the Jewish life in Kalisz. They took them out on the cemetery and they shot them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were without leadership. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Every day, they-- they-- like, they took me out to-- out of town. I had to stay a whole day and chop lumber. I never chopped lumber in my life. But I did. You didn't do it right, you didn't do enough, you got beaten up. You got hit. You got hit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people came home without-- an arm broken or broken leg. All these things happen every time. So every day, it was some-- some-- some disasters to families. So I-- and my older brother, which was married-- so I-- he went-- he-- he told us he's going to leave. He's going to Russia. At that time, the Russians let people go over the border into them. So he left. The younger-- the younger next to him, he left too. And they had a brother electrician. He left with his girlfriend.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was left with me and my younger brother. So I was-- my mother used to tell me, what are you going to do here? They're going to catch you every day, and you're going to-- who knows what will happen to you? Why didn't you go when your brothers went? So we got together with my brother. I got some bread, some clothing, whatever I had to speak of. And we went to the Russian border.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the Russian border, there was-- there was another tragedies. While we were riding with the-- with the train, you pass. Because they took off Jews from the train They took them to work and they disappeared. We were lucky. We got all the way to-- to-- to the-- to the river brook. This was the border between Germany and-- and-- and Poland, when Ribbentrop and the Russians made that-- they divided Poland in 1939. While we were standing there, there was a father and two daughters and-- and a wife. And the-- the-- the-- the peasants there, they had narrow boat-- canoes they have. So they used to carry over these people for-- for-- for money. For money, or for clothing, or for a watch. Whatever they-- they like, you had to give it to them because it's your life. They used to carry over the people in a canoe to the other side of the border, into-- into the Russian side. Sometimes, the Russians weren't there. They got through, and fine. But then what we saw, the girl went into the canoe. He-- he-- he was in the middle of the ocean, in the middle of the river. A Russian was there and shot it. She fell into the water. Imagine the screams and yells that mother and father, the daughter got drowned, killed in the water. It was a-- a swift water, just took her away. These are the kind of tragedies. There's another tragedy. When we crossed the border, they caught us-- me and my brother, already, away-- away from-- from-- from-- from the river. And they took us to a place they called [NON-ENGLISH]. There were already several hundred people there that they caught during the day. But thousands and thousands of Jews were trying to get away. And we were talking to each other, you know, a whole group of people. All of a sudden comes out a soldier, another five, six behind him. And they're dividing that group.

speaker: interviewee
text: So for example, I-- I was here. My brother was on the other side. He was in the other group. So they-- they told us, this group will go to Russia, and the other goes back to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now it's-- it's unimaginable how people start, try to beg them. Look, this is my wife. Let me go to Germany with her. The other, like my brother was on the other side there, said, let me go with my brother. Nothing doing-- like, you take a piece of bread and cut it in half and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no feeling. No, this what-- this what the people that they want to give a way of life for the free world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have anything with you? Did you have papers, passports, other than the-- the bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, nothing. I had a pair of shoes, a pair of pants, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were not able to take anything with you. What happened-- you left with your brother. And I know your sister was--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel. She was in Israel already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I told you, we thought that they wouldn't bother old people. This is just for us. And then we thought that maybe this is just in the beginning. You know, they're trying to-- to subjugate the people, tying to scare them. And so, you know-- and this will go away. Maybe one commander, the-- the chief there, he wants to show something. It'll go away. That's why we went. We thought it's going to be a temporary thing to go into Russia, and then we come back. But that didn't happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was permanent. My mother was left, and she wind up in the Warsaw ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to her there?

speaker: interviewee
text: She died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she die from illness, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who knows? Who knows? When I was in Russia, I used to send her packages, whatever I could. I was going hungry there, too, because I had to save up for the packages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if she ever received them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she received them. I have letters here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were writing to your mother when she was in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year she was taken to the Warsaw ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: '41, I think-- 40-- I-- I don't-- I didn't look at-- I have a whole bunch of cards-- not letters, but cards that we were allowed to write. So she wrote me, if I can send a coffee or tea. These things probably were very expensive those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: When she lived in the Warsaw ghetto, who did she live with? The family wasn't with her anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't live with nobody. They take 10 people this room, 20 people in the other room. That's all. You had no choice. There was no choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she write to you about the sanitary conditions there, or the cooking facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, a mother never writes to a child that she's suffering. But I know. We knew. We knew. And then stopped the writing, completely was-- it stopped when the Germans attacked Russia in 1941. Everything stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you really have no idea what happened to your mother, you just stopped hearing from her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, you're right. The war started, that's it. The whole correspondence was cut off.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's get back to you. You're in Russia now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your brother with you, or he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, OK. And where did you go in Russia at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we crossed the border, there's-- the first town was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]. Was a big town, big Jewish town. So I had another-- when I was in Warsaw, I had another to one Jew to go there. He said, over there, you can stay with him for a while. Somebody told me. And my younger brother was in the shul. There was-- you know, all the Jewish people congregate and you comes into a new place, you go to the shul there, you find-- you find your people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to this guy, and I told him I came from Warsaw and if I could sleep over. He said, yeah. So he gave-- there was a fireplace. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was-- was-- was a fireplace. And-- and he said, you can lay down here. He gave me a piece of old rag, and I should sleep on this piece of rag. So it's better than sleeping in the shul, or being in the street. So I was laying there. In the morning, I got up nice and quiet. I wash myself, and dress, and got sneaked out to the door so not to disturb them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, make the story short, after three days, he said, look. You cannot be here. We have no room for you, these kind of things. So I saw what-- what he-- what he means. So I went to the shul. And there we were sleeping like sardines. And there were thousands of people. There was no room! Sleeping or standing, or sleeping one next to the other-- I met a friend of mine. This was his honeymoon. He married-- the girl wouldn't go with him until he marry her. So he married her a day before crossing the border, his girlfriend. So they came into [NON-ENGLISH] And there he is on the-- on the floor. This is his honeymoon. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So after a while in [NON-ENGLISH], I found work in a bakery. So I worked in a bakery-- fine. We have bread already. My younger brother wanted to go further in. Because somehow, he found out-- I don't know how, so many years passed by already-- that he wanted to go to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to be a [NON-ENGLISH] in the town. So he left. And I was left all by myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my-- the one brother before the oldest, he-- somehow, he settled in a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, [NON-ENGLISH] where Jewish people-- not a shul, but there's people come and pray. So they settled there in the [NON-ENGLISH], and slept on the floor with a little straw. And-- and they slept there on the ground. And I was working-- working-- I got work in the bakery. I was young, you know. So I got to work in the bakery. And I work in the bakery, so fine. I got bread already and everything. But after a while, all these people had to leave the town because the [NON-ENGLISH] ordered up all the refugees, people that are not natives, they have to leave. They had to be 100 kilometers away from the border. I took a passport. You had to take a passport. Or you take a passport or they send you back to Germany. So I figured as long-- I'm a Polish citizen. Even if I have a Russian passport, if Poland's going to be again Poland, if Poland's going to be liberated, I probably always will be able to go back to Poland because I was born there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took that passport and-- and I went away. I found out that one uncle of mine is in Krzemieniec. This is-- this is not far from Lvov, if you know the town, Lvov. Not far from Lvov. So I went there. And he worked in a bakery. Because the whole family, as I told you, my mother's family, all the brothers and everything were bakers. So he worked already in a bakery. I came in there. He found me a job in the bakery. So I worked in the-- in the-- in the bakery there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there, more or less, life was already normal. Everybody worked. The Russians, they gave everybody-- everybody was supplied with-- with a job. After-- and-- and-- and the Russians knew that the Germans are getting ready to invade and to start war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- they took young people like us, they taught us with rifles, how to handle, how to handle grenades. Twice a week, they were teaching us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we passed a-- a-- a physical exam. So I had a rupture. So they sent me to the hospital, and was operated. After I got out of the hospital, I couldn't heavy-- carry the heavy stuff-- the breads and the flours, the sacks of flours. So they gave me a job in a pharmaceutical company. Not a company, was a distribution center, like a central place where everything comes in Kiev and they're distributing around in the area to the hospitals, to the-- to the pharmacies.

speaker: interviewee
text: There, I-- what I did-- what I did is scrub the floors. I was carrying boxes forth and back. There-- you know, this I have to tell you. The sabotage-- why the Russians behaved the way they behaved came in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Water from-- from-- from [NON-ENGLISH], water in bottles, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] water, you know. They shipped it in January. Until the water came in into-- into Krzemieniec, we had-- hundreds of bottles were busted, falling apart, because [NON-ENGLISH] when-- water, when freezes, expands.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all of us were processed. This was one case. Another case, sugar, you can keep it in no matter what degree. It won't disintegrate or melt away except if you put it between water. So sugar, they sent by airplane from Kiev to-- to-- to Krzemieniec by airplane. And then it was a typhus epidemic. So the-- the-- the-- the-- the-- to fight the typhus, the-- the anti bacteria medicine, they send it by train. Till they came, all was gone. This is the sabotage, you know, the [NON-ENGLISH] sabotage. Anyway, so we-- we were there, I working at the pharmaceutical company, my uncle who worked in the bakery. And the-- the war started in 1941, the Russian German war. So I was greeting the Germans again, you know? The second time, they were liberating me, so to speak.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew what's all about. So I told to my uncle, the younger-- he has a brother that said, let's go over the border into Russian, the old border. And we had passports, everything. So we shouldn't have any problems. While we were going, we found out that all of these people that-- that don't have passports issued in Russia, they won't let them take-- they won't let-- they don't let them through the border. And the people that, like us, that took away the passports, you're-- you're nobody. And we had to go back. Had to wait, had no choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go back to at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Krzemieniec. Because we started-- we started out to go back into-- deep into Russia to escape the Germans. Because I knew already who they are, what to expect from them. So I was trying to run further.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- we got back. And we waited. But a day or two days, the Germans came in. The infantry came in. It wasn't too bad. They're just, Jude, Jude, you know, these kind of things. But physically, they didn't bother you match. But later on, when the administration got in, they settled down. They took all the-- the-- the-- the leaders from-- from town, and they shot them, all of them-- took them in the cemetery, shot them, all of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took the intelligentsia, like the-- the guy that was running the pharmaceutical base-- he was a pharmacist by himself. They took them out, him and his wife. They killed him. And the Ukrainian, they were liberated because Hitler promised them a land. [NON-ENGLISH], he was collaborating with-- with Hitler. And he promised them when he liberated the Ukraine, he's going to be the president or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Ukraine worked with the Germans. And the-- and the Germans themselves were, you know, they had the experience from Poland, and all these tricks. And so what-- what-- what happened, when the Germans came, they issued a order that Jews cannot walk on the sidewalk. They have to walk in the middle of the road. So that-- you already a Jew. You walk in the middle of the road, you're Jewish. You're fair game. And they-- what else? How did they know you were Jewish, though? I mean, I understand if you were walking in the middle of the road. But how did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: If they ever catch you-- like, my-- my friend. He didn't look like Jewish at all or something. Some were Ukrainian. But during the time that he worked with them, you know, from 1939 to '41, Jewish people associated with Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Ukraine, the Poles, saw who you are. So they knew already who you are, even if you look like a gentile. And you walking on the sidewalk, you got killed. They took you out and shot you right there as the example. Did you speak Russian at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I spoke Russian already. I spoke Russian. I-- I read already. Because you were-- 24 hours, it's swim or drown. Because only Russian was going on there. So I had to learn in a hurry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you had to walk in the middle of the road. They were catching us like a dog catcher catches dogs. That's how they were catching us. And there was a jail. The-- the Russians put in jail a lot of people there. When-- when the-- when the Germans came, they opened up the jail. And people run out. They took Jews out of-- on the outskirts of the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, they took two brothers. They had to dig a-- a ditch. One brother had to lay in. This I'm tell-- talking you from experience. One brother had to lay in, The other brother had to bury him alive. That's what they did. And all the Jews had to watch. Everybody-- had to be a-- a public show.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was another case-- they came up with such tricks to kill people, I don't know. There's another case that-- a father and a son, they had to have a father and a son. So they gave them a two by four, and the other guy a two by four. And they had to kill each other. How can a father kill-- hit a son? No, you're hitting hard enough. Hit harder and harder. The old man died. Old man died right there. They took people in the town where I was. There were one story house, like this house-- one story house. They put up 10, 15 people on-- on the roof. And the German went away about 50 yards, 70 yards, who knows what? In those days, you-- you had-- and there was shooting, like shooting-- like shooting off birds. That's what-- that's what they did. Did anybody try to escape from there at the time, seeing what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where are you going to escape? Where? The-- the-- the-- the-- the-- the Poles were against you, and the Ukraine were against you. There was nowhere. You go to the forest, you go to beg or something. I talk to people, have people, friends who were in the forest. As soon as they got in, you know what happened. The Germans put a-- a bottle of vodka or a kilo of salt for every Jew that they bring in. And there was shot vodka, they love it, you know. Salt-- was a shortage of salt. You rather can find gold than salt. So they-- they were anxious. They were sniffing out the Jew in order to get their kilo of salt or-- or a bottle of vodka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any news at this time of what was going on back in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. Not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any radios?

speaker: interviewee
text: None. I didn't have any radio. Other people-- but what-- in my case, one day, they caught me in the street. And-- and they took us to a place where there were-- bricks were laying there. And they had to even out our driveway. So we was sitting the whole day and breaking up the bricks, make them-- make them smaller and level off the driveway. And while we were working, we got kicked, hit over the head. This was summertime, I'm talking. There's a guy, wanted to have water. He asked the-- the-- the German if he can have some water. He said, yeah, take the pail and go get some water. So he drank water from the pail. While he was drinking water from the pail, he said, drink. You asked for water. Drink. He said, I have enough. No, drink. He drowned him in the pail. Going back to Kalisz, there was a-- a-- a director from Jewish Theater there, Levine. His son lives here. I met him. His father had to walk in in-- in-- into the river and drown himself. And all the Jews had to stay around and watch it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what happened to you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- in my case, let me go back what I wanted to talk to you. In my case, they caught me in the street and I was cutting-- breaking up these bricks for-- for the-- for the driver to straighten out. So one-- and this was in a company that they-- they were doing telephone connections from the front to-- to headquarters. They used to come with trucks forth and back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one driver came along and asked this German, says, give me one guy. So-- no, two or three guys I think he asked for. Anyway, I was between that-- that group. So we came over there. He showed us how to dismantle a head of flat tire. And those, the trucks, they had rings. Not like here-- we have by pressure. So the rings-- so he showed me how to do it. And I was handy, so I-- I did it. Then he told me to wash the truck, and I did it. Then we had to pump it up by hand, truck tires, which was a hard job to fill up the-- the-- the air. And then he saw that I'm handy. He said, you know what? You come tomorrow, come again. Because he had friends. So they-- I'm going to do for his friends, too. And-- and-- and while I was working there, he went for lunch. They had lunch. So he didn't eat the whole thing. So he brought back half of the-- his-- what do you call it? Army-- they have this pots and plants. What do you call it? They're carrying always with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so he brought back a half of his lunch. So they gave it to me because I worked fine. He gave me a piece of bread. He said, come on. Tomorrow, come again. So I say, you have to tell the guy that he should let me go. So he told him. Next day, I-- I-- I came again. And he-- he gave me to clean the car, wash up to truck, to wash the truck. And I worked with him. After a while, it looks like he went in-- they had a-- a repair shop. They owned a portable repair shop. Like, he had-- he had trucks. And he opened the both of the sides of the trucks, and becomes a big, big, huge platform. They have lathes there, electric welding, all this kind-- all this kind of things. When I got there, my friend from-- from-- which is in Canada, and I have another guy which lives in Israel now, they were working already there. So he went to this sergeant over there. He told him, I have a good Jew there, hardworking Jew. Maybe you can use him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came out and said, from today on, you're going to work here. So I said, good. Give me a paper. Because they're catching in the streets.
